findings.title: Worker pool index increment is not atomic across async workers
findings.description: runWorkerPool uses a shared `idx` variable incremented inside `while (idx < stocks.length) { const stock = stocks[idx++]; ... }`. While Node.js is single-threaded, the read-check-increment pattern is split across async boundaries. Specifically, the `idx % 100 === 0` manifest flush check uses the same shared `idx` after `idx++`, so multiple workers can simultaneously observe idx==100 (or simultaneously miss it), triggering duplicate manifest writes or skipping the flush entirely.
findings.mechanism: Worker A: reads idx (e.g. 99), assigns stock, idx becomes 100. Worker B: enters loop, reads idx==100, assigns stock, idx becomes 101 — before Worker A reaches `if (idx % 100 === 0)` it now sees 101 and skips the flush. Conversely if Worker A awaits before reaching the flush check, Workers B and C may also see idx==100/200/etc. The check should be against the post-increment value captured at assignment time, not the current shared idx.
findings.reproducer: Run with PULL_CONCURRENCY=20. The manifest flush at 100/200/... will trigger zero, one, or several times depending on scheduling — non-deterministic.
findings.impact: Incremental manifest write either fires multiple times (wasted IO, race with itself in writeFileSync — last write wins) or never fires at exactly the 100-ticker boundary. In the worst case a SIGKILL between two close-spaced flushes can leave an inconsistent manifest.
findings.suggested_fix: Capture the local index at assignment: `const myIdx = idx++; if (myIdx > 0 && myIdx % 100 === 0) writeManifestFn();`. Even better: make writeManifestIncremental idempotent by guarding with a mutex flag.

findings.title: Manifest write is not atomic — direct writeFileSync to _manifest.json
findings.description: Both writeManifestIncremental (line 593) and the final manifest write (lines 868-869) use `fs.writeFileSync(path, JSON.stringify(...))` directly. There is no tmp+rename pattern. If the process is SIGKILL'd mid-write (a real possibility given the GitHub Actions 165-min step timeout mentioned in the code), the manifest file is left truncated/corrupt. JSON.parse downstream then throws and the quality gate cannot read n_ok at all.
findings.mechanism: writeFileSync is non-atomic on POSIX. SIGKILL between the file open/truncate and the data flush leaves a zero-byte or partial file. Downstream readers (Verify Pull Coverage gate, score-aggregator, dashboard generators) call JSON.parse and crash, masking what was actually a successful pull of N tickers.
findings.reproducer: kill -9 the pull-yahoo process during a write — _manifest.json ends up empty/truncated. Also possible when two workers race the write at idx%100==0.
findings.impact: Workflow appears to have failed but actually produced valid snapshots. The Verify Pull Coverage gate may pass yesterday's stale data through, or fail outright because manifest is unparseable.
findings.suggested_fix: Use tmp+rename: `fs.writeFileSync(path+'.tmp', body); fs.renameSync(path+'.tmp', path);`. Rename is atomic on the same filesystem.

findings.title: Rate-limit retry timeout shorter than worst-case sleep — retries get aborted
findings.description: quoteSummaryWithRetry uses DELAYS=[5000,12000] and per-attempt timeout=12000ms. When Yahoo returns 429, the worker sleeps 5s, retries with another 12s timeout, sleeps 12s, retries again. Total worst-case time inside this function is 12+5+12+12+12 = 53s. But the comment claims `up to ~29s total`. More importantly: the `_withTimeout` wrapper races a 12s timer against quoteSummary — for slow-but-not-failing Yahoo responses (15-25s during regional outages), the function returns ETIMEDOUT which falls into errClass='timeout' rather than triggering a retry. There is no retry for timeout errors.
findings.mechanism: Line 566: `isRateLimit = /429|too many request|rate.?limit/i.test(...)`. Timeouts thrown by `_withTimeout` have message 'ETIMEDOUT after 12000ms (...)' which doesn't match the rate-limit regex, so attempt loop exits immediately with `throw e`. A flaky Yahoo response that succeeds at 13s is permanently classified as failure even though one retry would succeed.
findings.reproducer: During Yahoo regional latency spikes (common around US market open), p95 quoteSummary latency exceeds 12s. The first attempt times out, no retry happens, ticker fails with errClass='timeout'.
findings.impact: Higher fail-ratio during latency spikes. A latency-induced 20% timeout rate trips the 75% fail-ratio gate at line 913 with cascading failures.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) raise the timeout to 30s for first attempt and 15s for retries (asymmetric), or (b) also retry on timeout class. Currently neither is done — the function silently treats slow responses as terminal.

findings.title: Yahoo incomeStatementHistory schema drift not handled in mapper
findings.description: Code comments (line 391-392) admit `Yahoo's incomeStatementHistory Submodule liefern seit Nov 2024 fast nichts.` The mapper still calls `_arr(isHist, 'totalRevenue')` and `_arr(isHist, 'operatingIncome')` etc. against this dead source, then relies on FTS fallback at line 716-733. The fallback uses `.length > X.length` comparisons to decide which wins — but when the quoteSummary version returns an array of length 4 with all-null values stripped by `.filter(Boolean)`, length comparisons no longer reflect data quality. Multi-fallback at lines 290-294 covers cash (`cash || cashAndCashEquivalents || cashAndShortTermInvestments`) but the same drift affects other fields (e.g. `operatingIncome` was renamed `totalOperatingIncomeAsReported` in some periods; `netIncome` partial replacement by `netIncomeContinuousOperations`) — the mapper does not multi-fallback for these.
findings.mechanism: isHist often returns rows with null totalRevenue but non-null operatingIncome. `_arr` filters all out via `.filter(Boolean)`. The FTS override `if (ftsAnnual.annualOpInc.length > 0) canonical.annual.annualOpInc = ftsAnnual.annualOpInc;` always overrides, but this only catches operatingIncome — netIncome only overrides `if (ftsAnnual.annualNetIncome.length > canonical.annual.annualNetIncome.length)`. If FTS returns 3 years and quoteSummary returns 4 stale/junk years, the bad data wins.
findings.reproducer: Pick a non-US large-cap; observe annualNetIncome populated from stale quoteSummary instead of fresher FTS values.
findings.impact: Methods consuming annualNetIncome (piotroski-f-score, sloan-ratio, earnings-stability) get stale or inconsistent data without warning. Silent corruption.
findings.suggested_fix: Either always prefer FTS when non-empty, or compare on non-null value-count rather than array length. Add explicit field-fallback list for operatingIncome/netIncome to match the cash fallback pattern.

findings.title: FTS override comparison uses .length but FTS arrays can be 0-length on partial failure
findings.description: fetchFundamentalsTS wraps each FTS call in try/catch and returns an empty array `[]` on failure (line 398: `out.annualFin = []`). The override logic uses `if (ftsAnnual.annualOpInc.length > 0)` and overwrites canonical.annual.annualOpInc — but if FTS failed partially (e.g. annual financials succeeded, cash-flow failed), `ftsAnnual.annualFCF` is `[]` AND `ftsAnnual.annualOpInc` has length > 0. The `if (ftsAnnual.annualFCF.length > 0) canonical.annual.annualFCF = ftsAnnual.annualFCF;` does NOT execute when FCF is empty — but the cached payload at line 711-712 will then store empty FCF arrays for 28 days. Re-runs hit the cache, never see the failure flag, and the snapshot keeps the original quoteSummary FCF indefinitely with no provenance trail.
findings.mechanism: Line 696-714: if FTS partially fails, the cache stores the partial result. Cache TTL is 28 days. For 28 days subsequent runs use `useCache=true` and never retry the failed module. The snapshot retains the (potentially stale or worse — possibly mixed) result.
findings.reproducer: Force `yf.fundamentalsTimeSeries(symbol, {...module:'cash-flow'})` to reject. Re-run; cache returns empty annualFCF persistently for 28 days.
findings.impact: FCF-based methods (fcf-yield, ev-ebitda when EBITDA derives from FCF, premium-compounder-proof) silently lose data for 4 weeks per affected ticker. No surface indicator the data is stale.
findings.suggested_fix: Track per-module success in the cache; only cache when all modules succeed, OR cache with TTL=1d when any module failed. Add `_ftsPartial: true` flag to snapshot when this occurs.

findings.title: errClass switch has no default-pass — 'other' bucket conflates real bugs with transient
findings.description: errClass starts as 'other' and is only overwritten if msg matches one of 5 patterns: rate-limit, not-found, timeout, network, parse. Anything else (TypeError reading undefined, ReferenceError, ENOSPC disk-full, ENAMETOOLONG, EACCES) falls into 'other'. Programming errors (e.g. malformed Yahoo response causing `cur[k]` at line 233 to crash) become indistinguishable from real Yahoo failures and don't surface in monitoring.
findings.mechanism: Lines 799-805 cascade through if/elif. The 'parse' branch (`/parse|unexpected token|JSON/i`) won't catch a `TypeError: Cannot read property 'X' of undefined` raised inside mapper. Such errors land in 'other' bucket which check-pull-stats.js (referenced via 'pull-stats-check' in the comment) presumably treats as unknown/benign.
findings.reproducer: Inject a stock with malformed yahoo response (e.g. `yahoo = { summaryDetail: null }` — then `_y(sd, 'marketCap')` works because _y handles null, but `(isHist || [])` is fine — most code is defensive. However `q.regularMarketPrice` access at line 625 has no null guard when `existing.price` is missing.
findings.impact: Bug in the mapper logic looks identical to a transient Yahoo blip. Operators can't tell whether to push a code fix or wait for Yahoo to recover.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'data-shape' or 'mapper-bug' class catching TypeError/ReferenceError specifically. Also log the stack for 'other' errors so they're debuggable post-mortem.

findings.title: Price-only update can lose currency information
findings.description: _priceOnlyUpdate sets `existing.price.regularMarketPrice = q.regularMarketPrice` and `existing.price.currency = q.currency || existing.price.currency`. But the existing snapshot has been FX-converted to USD at the previous full pull. The new q.regularMarketPrice from yf.quote() returns the value in *Yahoo's reporting currency* (e.g. EUR for European stocks, GBp for some UK shares). This is written into existing.price.regularMarketPrice without any FX conversion. Downstream code reading existing.price treats it as USD because meta.reportingCurrency was overwritten to 'USD' during the prior full pull.
findings.mechanism: Prior full pull: marketCap converted to USD, meta.reportingCurrency='USD'. Today's price-only path: yf.quote returns regularMarketPrice in EUR (e.g. 50.00), writes 50.00 into existing.price.regularMarketPrice, and similarly marketCap is overwritten with the raw EUR/local mcap value `existing.marketCap.value = q.marketCap` (line 632). This breaks USD invariant established by line 218.
findings.reproducer: Pull a European stock fully on day 0 (snapshot stored in USD); 6 days later price-only path runs, q.marketCap arrives in EUR, written into snapshot, snapshot now mixes USD annual data with EUR marketCap.
findings.impact: Silent ~16% currency mismatch on all non-USD stocks in price-only mode. Affects fcf-yield, ev-ebitda, every ratio comparing marketCap or revenue against annual values. Worst case for high-FX-rate currencies (JPY ~150x, KRW ~1300x).
findings.suggested_fix: Apply FX in price-only path too — convert q.marketCap/q.regularMarketPrice to USD using existing.meta.fxRateApplied or re-derive from currency. OR: only run price-only fast-path for stocks where existing.meta.reportingCurrencyOriginal === 'USD'.

findings.title: _convertSnapshotToUSD mutates input — second call doubles the FX conversion
findings.description: _convertSnapshotToUSD mutates the snapshot in place: `snap.marketCap = scale(snap.marketCap)`, `snap.meta.reportingCurrency = 'USD'`. If this function is ever called twice on the same snapshot (e.g. price-only path then full path re-runs in same process), the second call sees meta.reportingCurrency='USD' and returns early — but a caller that bypasses this guard (or if the early-return logic at line 164 is ever weakened) would scale by the FX rate twice. More immediately, _quality grading at line 768 runs AFTER _convertSnapshotToUSD; if grade fails it sets `_quality = { grade: 'D', ...}` but the snapshot is already mutated, so partial state on the disk can mix converted+pristine views.
findings.mechanism: The function takes single-pass through annual/timeseries and multiplies by `factor`. Idempotency relies entirely on the meta.reportingCurrency='USD' early-return. If a future refactor allows reconversion (e.g. forcing re-pull on cached snapshot), values would be silently squared.
findings.reproducer: Manually call _convertSnapshotToUSD(snap) twice — second call returns early due to currency=='USD'. But change meta.reportingCurrency back to 'EUR' and re-call: values are now scaled twice with no warning.
findings.impact: Fragile idempotency. The same risk applies if any code path resets `meta.reportingCurrency` without also resetting numeric values — silent 1.08^2 = 1.17x corruption for EUR stocks.
findings.suggested_fix: Make _convertSnapshotToUSD pure (return new object). Add a `meta.fxConverted = true` flag distinct from currency, and bail on that instead of inspecting currency string.

findings.title: FX_TO_USD missing PLN and TRY mapped to fallback table — refresh-fx.js doesn't pull them
findings.description: CURRENCIES list in refresh-fx.js: ['EUR', 'GBP', 'CHF', 'SEK', 'NOK', 'DKK', 'JPY', 'HKD', 'CNY', 'AUD', 'CAD', 'KRW', 'INR', 'TWD', 'BRL', 'MXN', 'ZAR', 'SGD']. But pull-yahoo's REGION_BY_CURRENCY maps PLN, GBp, RUB, TRY, SAR, AED, ILS, THB, IDR, NZD to regions. None of these (other than those overlapping) are refreshed. PLN/THB/IDR/NZD are silently absent from FX_TO_USD (only hardcoded fallback). For currencies not in FX_FALLBACK, line 177 returns null and sets fxConversionFailed=true, so marketCap stays in local currency.
findings.mechanism: FX_FALLBACK has 19 currencies (lines 87-94). REGION_BY_CURRENCY references currencies not in FX_FALLBACK: PLN, NZD, THB, IDR, RUB, TRY, SAR, AED, ILS — these stocks always fail FX conversion and stay in local currency. Their marketCap then fails the `MIN_MCAP = 1e9 USD` filter incorrectly (a 10B PLN mcap looks like $10B USD).
findings.reproducer: Pull a Polish (.WA) stock with marketCap=4e9 PLN (~$1B). Snapshot stays in PLN, mcap filter at line 748 sees 4e9 and accepts as 'mid-cap' — but actual USD value is $1B. Methods see $4B and may misclassify size.
findings.impact: Region/currency miscategorization for stocks in unsupported currencies. Worse for high-FX rates: 100B IDR mcap ($6M USD) bypasses the $1B floor because IDR isn't in FX_FALLBACK.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add PLN/NZD/THB/IDR/RUB/TRY/SAR/AED/ILS to both FX_FALLBACK and refresh-fx CURRENCIES, OR drop these from REGION_BY_CURRENCY so they don't enter the universe.

findings.title: Refresh-FX writes failed currencies as silent gap — old rate persists
findings.description: When fetchFXRate(c) returns null, the code does NOT delete rates[c] — it leaves the previous value from `existing` (loaded line 34). So if today's JPY pull fails, yesterday's JPY rate is kept under fetchedAt=today. pull-yahoo's staleness check at line 105 (`ageDays > FX_STALE_DAYS`) then accepts the file as fresh, but JPY rate is actually stale. failed[] is logged but not surfaced to the snapshot.
findings.mechanism: Line 36: `const rates = Object.assign({ USD: 1.0 }, existing);` — pre-seeds with old values. Loop at 38-47: only overwrites on success. failed list is included in the JSON but pull-yahoo (line 102-112) never reads `failed` and treats all keys uniformly.
findings.reproducer: Force one currency fetch to fail. Inspect fx-rates.json: that currency's rate is the old value, fetchedAt is today, no surface indication it's stale.
findings.impact: Stale FX rate goes undetected for weeks. The 'failed' field in fx-rates.json is dead documentation. Drift accumulates silently.
findings.suggested_fix: In pull-yahoo loadFx, check raw.failed[] and drop those currencies from FX_TO_USD (forcing fxConversionFailed=true downstream). Also, set rates[c] = null in refresh-fx on failure so the row is obviously broken.

findings.title: Mcap-filter log message says $2B/$300B but constants are $1B/Infinity
findings.description: MIN_MCAP=1e9 and MAX_MCAP=Infinity (lines 745-746), but the human-readable log message hard-codes `< $2B (Small-Cap)` and `> $300B (Mega-Cap)`. These thresholds are stale comments from an earlier version. The reason string is what's persisted in the manifest under `results.reason` — any downstream tool that filters/reports on these strings will misclassify which stocks were rejected at what threshold.
findings.mechanism: Line 749: `const reason = mcapVal < MIN_MCAP ? 'mcap=...B < $2B (Small-Cap)' : 'mcap=...B > $300B (Mega-Cap)';`. Since MAX_MCAP=Infinity, the second branch is dead code (mcapVal > Infinity is always false). All rejections hit the first branch with the stale '$2B' label.
findings.reproducer: Run pull and inspect _manifest-full.json. Every skipped-mcap entry will say '< $2B' even though the actual threshold is $1B.
findings.impact: Misleading diagnostics in manifest. Operators reviewing rejections see '$2B' threshold and may compute wrong skip rates. Confuses prune logic if it ever parses these reasons.
findings.suggested_fix: Use template strings: `reason = mcap=...B < $${(MIN_MCAP/1e9).toFixed(0)}B`. Also remove dead Mega-Cap branch since MAX_MCAP=Infinity.

findings.title: writeManifestIncremental can race itself across workers
findings.description: Multiple workers can reach `if (idx % 100 === 0) writeManifestFn()` near-simultaneously (see F-DP-001). Two parallel writeFileSync calls to the same path: Node serializes the writes but the second overwrites the first; if either is interrupted mid-stream the file may be corrupt. Additionally results.length and failures.length are read without coordination, so the snapshot is non-atomic — a worker just-pushed-to-results between read of results.length and failures.length yields an off-by-one count.
findings.mechanism: Both arrays are mutated by multiple workers concurrently. Reading their lengths to build the manifest is non-atomic w.r.t the rest of the writers. Not strictly a TOCTOU bug since JS is single-threaded, but ordering across async boundaries means n_ok+n_failed != n_processed momentarily.
findings.reproducer: Hard to trigger but observable under sustained high concurrency where flush boundary aligns with a results.push.
findings.impact: Off-by-one in incremental manifest counts; cosmetic, low-impact.
findings.suggested_fix: Compute snapshot atomically: `const r=results.length, f=failures.length; const slim={...,n_ok:r, n_failed:f};`. Use tmp+rename (see F-DP-002).

findings.title: Yahoo-finance2 queue concurrency stacked with worker pool — actual concurrency is 2x
findings.description: Line 58 creates yf with `queue: { concurrency: _YF_CONC }` where _YF_CONC=PULL_CONCURRENCY (default 10). Line 547 sets CONCURRENCY=PULL_CONCURRENCY=10 for the outer worker pool. Both are 10. Each outer worker spawns up to (quoteSummary + quote + 4x fundamentalsTimeSeries) = 6 yf HTTP calls per ticker. The yf-internal queue allows 10 simultaneous HTTP requests across all workers — but outer workers wait sequentially for their own ticker's yf calls. The result: yf-queue is the actual rate limiter, but the outer pool effectively serializes per-worker. PULL_CONCURRENCY=20 would saturate the yf-queue at 10, with 10 workers idle. The rate-limit comment (line 833) saying '20 workers and rateLimitMs=1500ms per worker, throughput is ~13 tickers/sec' is wrong: at most 10 yf requests fly at once.
findings.mechanism: Two-layer concurrency: outer pool of N workers, inner queue of M slots. Effective throughput = min(N, M/calls-per-ticker). With N=10 and M=10 and 6 calls per ticker, only ~1.6 tickers can be in-flight simultaneously.
findings.reproducer: Set PULL_CONCURRENCY=30 and observe HTTP request rate via tcpdump — never exceeds 10/sec.
findings.impact: Pull throughput is bottlenecked at the internal queue, not the outer pool. Increasing PULL_CONCURRENCY has zero effect once it equals the queue limit. Total pull duration is ~6x longer than rate-limit gating implies. Documented throughput in comments is wrong.
findings.suggested_fix: Decouple: outer pool concurrency = M (e.g. 10), yf-queue concurrency = N * (calls-per-ticker) where N is the desired number of in-flight tickers. OR use a single layer and document clearly.

findings.title: rateLimitMs sleep between tickers is per-worker but yf-queue is global
findings.description: Each worker sleeps `rateLimitMs` (default 1500ms) between tickers. With 10 workers, the effective inter-request interval at the global level is 150ms — far below Yahoo's tolerance. The intent of the rate-limit guard is undermined by the worker-pool structure. Worse: this 1500ms idle wait happens AFTER each ticker's 6 yf calls, so workers spend most of their time sleeping individually rather than helping the pool drain.
findings.mechanism: Worker A finishes ticker, sleeps 1.5s, picks next ticker. Worker B-J doing same independently. Global request rate = workers × (1/1.5) = 6.67 req/s × 6 calls = ~40 req/s. Yahoo's 429 threshold is typically ~10-20 req/s sustained.
findings.reproducer: Run with default 1500ms and 10 workers, check rate-limit failures. Likely >5% rate-limit class even at idle hours.
findings.impact: Self-inflicted rate-limiting. Tier-2 confidence: actual Yahoo limits are not documented, but the asymmetry between per-worker and global is real.
findings.suggested_fix: Move sleep to a global token-bucket (or rely solely on yf-queue with configurable per-call delay). Or set rateLimitMs = total_target_ms × CONCURRENCY.

findings.title: Discovery sources merge without preserving 'source' attribution
findings.description: Lines 245-253: when merging a discovery source result, the merge omits the `source` field that each discovery module sets (e.g. 'sec-edgar', 'finnhub', 'nasdaq-trader'). Line 289-297 writes to wlRaw with `added_via: info.source || 'auto-universe-refresh'` — but info here is from allTickers Map values which never had `source` set. Result: every newly added ticker is tagged 'auto-universe-refresh' regardless of which discovery source surfaced it. The prune script can't differentiate dead Finnhub additions from Wikipedia ones, and no rollback path exists when one source returns junk.
findings.mechanism: Loop at 243-254 constructs `{ ticker, marketCap, name, sector, exchange }` — drops `source`. Then line 295: `added_via: info.source || 'auto-universe-refresh'` always resolves to the fallback.
findings.reproducer: Run refresh-universe; inspect any new ticker in watchlist.json: added_via='auto-universe-refresh' even for SEC-EDGAR-sourced tickers.
findings.impact: Loss of provenance. When a discovery source breaks (e.g. SEC returns a fake ticker), there's no audit trail to remove just those rows.
findings.suggested_fix: Include `source: info.source` in the merge at line 245-253. Also surface multi-source attribution (a ticker may appear in both SEC and NASDAQ).

findings.title: Universe-cap by marketCap rank silently drops tickers without marketCap hint
findings.description: When allTickers.size > MAX_UNIVERSE (13000), entries are sorted `(a, b) => (b[1].marketCap || 0) - (a[1].marketCap || 0)` and truncated. Tickers from SEC/Finnhub/Wikipedia/NASDAQ-Trader have `marketCap: null` (they don't provide it). These get sorted to the bottom and dropped first. The implicit assumption is that mcap-less sources are 'less valuable' — but they include legitimate large-caps that Yahoo screener missed. Effectively the universe cap discards exactly the tickers added for coverage.
findings.mechanism: Map merge at line 248 sets `marketCap: info.marketCap || null` — only nasdaq-api provides marketCap from Yahoo screener. The other 5 sources (SEC, Finnhub, Wikipedia, NASDAQ-Trader, OTC) all return null. So when cap triggers, all-null-mcap entries land at the end of the sort and are deleted.
findings.reproducer: Force allTickers.size = 14000 (e.g. set MAX_UNIVERSE=8000). Inspect which tickers are dropped — disproportionately from SEC/Finnhub/Wikipedia.
findings.impact: Discovery sources added for coverage are silently the first to be cut. Defeats the purpose of multi-source discovery.
findings.suggested_fix: Sort with explicit fallback: tickers with no mcap should be ranked by a heuristic (presence in multiple sources, sector hint exists, etc.). OR fetch a cheap yf.quote() for null-mcap tickers to populate marketCap before ranking. OR cap each source's contribution independently.

findings.title: OTC fetchOTCMarkets pageSize check stops at first short page — may miss data
findings.description: Line 151: `if (rows.length < PAGE_SIZE) { break; }`. OTC API may return a partial page in the middle of paginated results (e.g. server-side filtering, transient hiccup). Stopping at first short page loses subsequent pages. The totalRecords check at line 155 only fires if total was reported on a prior page.
findings.mechanism: If page N returns 499 of 500 due to a stale row being filtered server-side, the loop breaks even though page N+1 still has data. Combined with page-1 not reporting total (some shapes don't), no fallback exists.
findings.reproducer: Mock OTC API to return [499, 500, 500] in pages — first page break aborts second/third pages.
findings.impact: Coverage loss in OTC tier. Bounded since MAX_PAGES=10 caps total, but unpredictable.
findings.suggested_fix: Continue pagination until empty page (rows.length === 0), not partial. Track consecutive short pages.

findings.title: Wikipedia ticker regex over-permissive, includes false positives
findings.description: Regex `/^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]{0,5}$/` matches 1-6 char uppercase strings. Wikipedia tables routinely contain non-ticker text matching this: 'CEO', 'USA', 'GAAP', 'IPO', 'NYSE', 'CIK', 'SEC' all match. These get treated as tickers, added to watchlist, and produce failed pull-yahoo lookups (404 errClass).
findings.mechanism: Wikitext parsing splits cells by pipe. Header text like 'Symbol' is filtered (doesn't start with uppercase digit followed by uppercase mix), but abbreviations in 'Notes' columns slip through.
findings.reproducer: Run fetchWikipediaIndices() — inspect output, look for non-ticker shorthand like 'CEO', 'IPO'.
findings.impact: Pollutes universe with junk tickers. Each junk ticker costs ~1 quoteSummary call before failing 404. ~50-100 junk entries possible per index.
findings.suggested_fix: Tighten regex (require digit, e.g. AAPL not AAA) or cross-validate against a known-ticker list. Or restrict to the first column of each row only.

findings.title: Cached FTS payload survives schema changes — no version key
findings.description: fundamentals-cache stores `{ cachedAt, payload: { ftsAnnual, ftsQuarterly, ftsBalance, ftsAnnualSBC, ftsAnnualCapex, ftsQuarterlyNI } }`. No schema version is stored. If the mapper (mapFTSToAnnual, mapFTSToBalance) is updated to extract a new field, cached entries from prior code still serve the old shape for up to 28 days. Re-deployments don't invalidate the cache.
findings.mechanism: Line 678-686 loads cached payload by file path and timestamp only. New field additions to mapFTSToBalance (e.g. adding `currentLiabilities`) are inherited from the latest pull — cached rows return undefined for new fields. score-aggregator may treat undefined as missing-data and degrade.
findings.reproducer: Add a new field extraction in mapFTSToAnnual, deploy. Tickers with valid cache continue returning the old shape until cache TTL expires.
findings.impact: Silent stale data when schemas evolve. Affects any new field the engine needs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_cacheVersion: 'v1'` to cache payload; bump on mapper changes. Invalidate cache when version mismatch.

findings.title: _ftsExtractByYear returns values without year metadata — breaks year-based methods
findings.description: The function name and JSDoc say `Returns [{year: 2025, value: ...}, ...] sorted latest first`. The actual implementation `out.push(v)` pushes the raw value, not a {year, value} object. Then line 702-703: `ftsAnnualSBC = _ftsExtractByYear(fts.annualCash, ['stockBasedCompensation']);` is assigned to canonical.annual.annualSBC at line 723. Engine methods expecting year-tagged data (e.g. capex-trend, sbc-revenue) get raw numbers and have no way to align them by year against annualRev — they rely on positional alignment that breaks the first time FTS skips a year due to null.
findings.mechanism: Yahoo FTS returns rows oldest-first. Reverse + filter null. Each kept row pushes its raw value. If 2023 is null but 2022 and 2024 are valid, the resulting array is [2024_val, 2022_val] with no indicator that 2023 was skipped. Methods doing `annualRev[i] / annualSBC[i]` will compute wrong-year ratios.
findings.reproducer: Pick a ticker with sparse SBC reporting (small-caps); annualRev has 4 years, annualSBC has 3, indices misalign.
findings.impact: Silent off-by-one in year-aligned ratios for methods consuming annualSBC and annualCapex. Affects sbc-revenue and capex-trend.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) include year in the output, or (b) preserve sparseness (push null instead of filtering). Engine code needs to expect year tags too.

findings.title: Annual arrays from FTS lose year alignment after filter(Boolean) / filter null
findings.description: mapFTSToAnnual, mapFTSToBalance, mapFTSToQuarterly all `continue` when the primary field (totalRevenue, etc.) is null but emit a row when present. Secondary fields (operatingIncome, grossProfit) are pushed *only if non-null*. This means annualRev[0..3] and annualOpInc[0..3] may correspond to *different years*. Methods doing op-margin = annualOpInc[i] / annualRev[i] silently compute cross-year ratios.
findings.mechanism: Line 444-451: if `rev != null`, push to annualRev unconditionally. Push to annualOpInc only `if (oi != null)`. Same pattern for grossProfit, netIncome. After 5 years where year 3 has null OI: annualRev has 5 elements, annualOpInc has 4 — index alignment broken. Year 3's revenue indexed at [2] is paired with year 4's OI also at [2].
findings.reproducer: Pick a stock with 1 year of missing OI in FTS. annualRev and annualOpInc lengths differ. revenue-growth methods see a phantom year.
findings.impact: Year-misaligned computations across margin/profitability/efficiency methods. Affects op-margin, gross-margin-stability, net-debt-ebitda when EBITDA derives from OI, ROIC.
findings.suggested_fix: Either push null placeholders to preserve positional alignment OR include year tags and require methods to align by year. Same fix as F-DP-020.

findings.title: prune-watchlist treats missing snapshot as 'keep' indefinitely
findings.description: isDeadSnapshot returns false when no snapshot exists ('not dead yet'). A ticker added to watchlist that pull-yahoo never successfully pulls (e.g. due to persistent 404) accumulates failure entries in _manifest but never gets a snapshot file, so prune leaves it indefinitely. The watchlist grows monotonically with junk.
findings.mechanism: Line 49: `if (!fs.existsSync(fp)) return null;`. Line 55: `if (!snap) return false; // no snapshot = not dead yet`. There's no upper bound on how long a ticker can stay without a snapshot.
findings.reproducer: Add a typo ticker (e.g. 'XYXYXYX') to watchlist; pull-yahoo fails 404 daily; prune never removes it.
findings.impact: Watchlist bloat with permanently invalid tickers. Each costs one quoteSummary attempt per pull.
findings.suggested_fix: Track per-ticker consecutive failure count in the manifest (or a separate file). Prune when count exceeds threshold (e.g. 7 daily fails in a row).

findings.title: prune-watchlist reads snapshot.meta.delisted without null-check after isDeadSnapshot returns true
findings.description: Line 95: `const reason = (snap.meta && snap.meta.delisted) ? 'delisted' : 'stale+no-data';` — snap is guaranteed non-null at this point (isDeadSnapshot returned true), but the second pathway (stale+no-data) reports 'stale+no-data' even when snap.meta.delisted is undefined. This is technically correct but the labeling can be misleading if a future snapshot omits meta.delisted on a genuinely-delisted ticker.
findings.mechanism: Only minor — reason categorization is purely cosmetic. The bigger issue: when isDeadSnapshot returns true with delisted=true, but the snapshot's meta was somehow truncated, the reason mislabels.
findings.reproducer: Manually edit a snapshot to remove meta.delisted but keep all the empty-financial-data conditions. Prune would call it 'stale+no-data' rather than recognizing the delisted intent.
findings.impact: Cosmetic mislabel in prune logs. Low priority.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass the dead-reason from isDeadSnapshot rather than re-deriving in main loop.

findings.title: FX_FALLBACK rates dated to 2024 — drift up to 16% by 2026
findings.description: FX_FALLBACK hardcodes rates that look like late-2023/early-2024 levels (EUR=1.08, JPY=0.0067, GBP=1.27, etc.). With FX rates having moved significantly by 2026 (EUR/USD has traded 1.00-1.15 range), the fallback is materially stale. If fx-rates.json is missing or >14 days old, snapshots silently convert with up to 16% wrong FX.
findings.mechanism: Lines 87-94 are literal numeric constants. The comment on line 86 explicitly acknowledges they 'kann Monate stale sein — flagged via _log WARN' — but the WARN is logged on load, not persisted in the snapshot. Once snapshot is written, the bad FX is baked in until next pull.
findings.reproducer: Delete fx-rates.json. Run pull. All non-USD stocks use hardcoded 2024 rates.
findings.impact: Up to 16% silent currency error across all ratios for non-USD stocks during periods when fx-rates.json is unavailable (e.g. refresh-fx workflow failure, or first-ever run before fx-rates.json exists).
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) make FX hard-coded fallback an absolute failure (refuse to convert, set fxConversionFailed=true) so downstream knows, or (b) refresh the fallback values from a periodic audit. Add explicit `_fxFallbackUsed: true` flag in snapshot.

findings.title: Shared cacheDir mkdirSync race between concurrent workers
findings.description: Inside processOne (called concurrently by N workers), line 675: `if (!fs.existsSync(cacheDir)) fs.mkdirSync(cacheDir, { recursive: true });`. Worker A and B both run existsSync at same time, both see false, both call mkdirSync. The second mkdirSync with recursive:true should succeed silently due to EEXIST handling — but the more concerning issue is the same pattern applies to outputDir at top of pullAll (line 543) which is OK because pullAll runs once. The per-ticker case is benign but indicates careless concurrent IO.
findings.mechanism: fs.mkdirSync recursive:true does swallow EEXIST so no crash. But it's wasteful and pattern-positive — if a future refactor uses non-recursive mkdir, the race becomes a real bug.
findings.reproducer: Delete fundamentals-cache dir; run with PULL_CONCURRENCY=20. All 20 workers race to create the dir.
findings.impact: None today. Pattern-match T3 concern: similar race in any other code path that doesn't use recursive:true.
findings.suggested_fix: Hoist mkdirSync to outside processOne (do it once at pullAll start). Not load-bearing today.

findings.title: First-trade-date pull silently swallows all errors — IPO year often missing
findings.description: The yf.quote() call to fetch firstTradeDateMilliseconds is wrapped in `try { ... } catch (e) { /* IPO-Feld optional, nicht-kritisch */ }` with a totally empty catch — no logging, no errClass, no flag in snapshot. When yf.quote() fails (timeout, rate-limit), the snapshot silently gets `firstTradeDate: null, ipoYear: null`. Methods that filter by IPO age (e.g. forecast-contamination-guard) cannot distinguish 'unknown IPO date' from 'genuinely old' tickers.
findings.mechanism: Line 671: empty catch swallows all errors silently. No retry, no log, no indicator in snapshot._quality. The 8s timeout (Tag 163 reduced from 15s) makes failure more likely under load.
findings.reproducer: Throttle yf.quote() to always time out. All snapshots have ipoYear=null without any error trace.
findings.impact: Forecast-contamination-guard and other IPO-age methods silently degrade. No surface signal in monitoring.
findings.suggested_fix: Log a warning at minimum. Optionally retry once with longer timeout, or carry through previous snapshot's ipoYear when available.

findings.title: ftsQuarterlyNI uses `var` redeclaration — second declaration is no-op
findings.description: Line 688: `var ftsQuarterlyNI;` at function scope. Line 705 inside else branch: `var ftsQuarterlyNI = (fts.quarterlyFin || []).slice()...`. Hoisting makes both declarations refer to the same variable. The assignment at 705 works, but the redundant `var` is a code-smell indicating the author may have intended block scope. Not a bug today, but if anyone refactors to `let`, the inner re-declaration would shadow and the cache path (line 695) and non-cache path (line 705) become disconnected.
findings.mechanism: JS var hoists to function scope. Two `var` declarations in same function are equivalent to one. Works today.
findings.reproducer: Refactor var→let on line 705 only; outer scope sees undefined.
findings.impact: Code-smell; no runtime bug currently.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the inner `var` on line 705; keep only the outer declaration.

findings.title: Snapshot delisted-flag write reads-then-writes without locking — race with concurrent processOne
findings.description: When errClass='not-found', the code reads the existing snapshot, sets meta.delisted=true, writes it back. This happens inside processOne for the failing ticker. If multiple workers simultaneously detect not-found for the same symbol (unlikely but possible during a re-run race), or if the price-only path concurrently wrote the same file, the read-modify-write can clobber. Less hypothetically: this write uses direct writeFileSync (no tmp+rename), same atomicity concern as F-DP-002.
findings.mechanism: Lines 813-817: readFileSync → mutate → writeFileSync. No fsync, no rename. SIGKILL between truncate and end-of-write corrupts the snapshot. Subsequent reads (downstream) get parse-error and treat the ticker as completely missing.
findings.reproducer: SIGKILL pull-yahoo during the delisted-flag write window — corrupted snapshot remains until next pull.
findings.impact: Rare data loss for the affected ticker. Combined with prune-watchlist's tolerance for missing snapshots (F-DP-022), this can keep a corrupted ticker around forever.
findings.suggested_fix: Use tmp+rename for this write too. See F-DP-002.
